EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system crossed the region, producing accumulating snowfall and gusty winds across much of western South Dakota. The highest snowfall amounts were across mostly far northwest South Dakota, where 2 to 4 inches fell during the overnight and morning hours. Gusty winds up to 50 mph developed during the day, producing areas of blowing snow and greatly reduced visibility at times.

An arctic air mass settled over the region for several days, producing extreme and dangerous wind chills across western and south-central South Dakota. Temperatures remained below zero for 3 days, as low as 20 below to 40 below zero across much of the area on the mornings of the 13th and 14th. Gusty north to northwest winds at times helped to produce wind chills in the 30 below to 70 below range from the morning of the 13th to the morning of the 15th.

Behind an arctic front, a narrow band of light to moderate snowfall and winds gusting from 35 to 55 mph resulted in visibility reductions of one-half mile or less. Travel conditions were briefly quite hazardous, but any blizzard or near blizzard conditions typically lasted for a couple of hours or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to one-half mile or less for roughly one to two hours due to a combination of wind gusts as high as 51 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Tyndall and snowfall which was less than an inch at Tyndall.  Travel conditions were poor during the lowest visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring storm system came through the region in several pieces, bringing a wide variety of weather to the area. Rain and snow developed initially with the first part of the system early on the 24th, with some freezing rain and a wintry mix across far southwest into south-central South Dakota. Snow was heaviest initially from the northern Black Hills area to northwest South Dakota, with heavier snow developing elsewhere to the southeast later on the 24th into the 25th. Gusty winds developed on the back side of the system across the western into central South Dakota plains, producing areas of blowing snow, with near blizzard conditions for a period on the 25th across far south-central South Dakota. The highest snowfall totals were generally from the Spearfish to Sturgis areas, and also across parts of northwest into central South Dakota, where 5 to 10 inches was mostly reported. Elsewhere across the northern Black Hills area and the west central South Dakota plains, amounts of 2 to 6 inches were reported in most areas.

An upper-level system moved across the region, bringing a couple of disturbances across the northern Plains. Accumulating snowfall with the first disturbance was mainly confined to the central and southern Black Hills on the 30th. A stronger disturbance moved across the area on the 31st into April 1st, and with colder air in place, produced a more widespread area of accumulating snowfall. Amounts of 2 to around 6 inches were common across much of western and south-central South Dakota, with at least localized higher amounts to around 10 inches from southwest into portions of south-central South Dakota.

A stronger disturbance moved across the area on the 31st into April 1st, and with colder air in place, produced a more widespread area of accumulating snowfall. Amounts of 2 to around 6 inches were common across much of western and south-central South Dakota, with at least localized higher amounts to around 10 inches from southwest into portions of south-central South Dakota.

An early spring storm system came through the region in several pieces, bringing a wide variety of weather to the area. Rain and snow developed initially with the first part of the system early on the 24th, with some freezing rain and a wintry mix across far southwest into south-central South Dakota. Snow was heaviest initially from the northern Black Hills area to northwest South Dakota, with heavier snow developing elsewhere to the southeast later on the 24th into the 25th. Gusty winds developed on the back side of the system across the western into central South Dakota plains, producing areas of blowing snow, with near blizzard conditions for a period on the 25th across far south-central South Dakota. The highest snowfall totals were generally from the Spearfish to Sturgis areas, and also across parts of northwest into central South Dakota, where 5 to 10 inches was mostly reported. Elsewhere across the northern Black Hills area and the west central South Dakota plains, amounts of 2 to 6 inches were reported in most areas.

A broad area of low pressure deepened as it drifted between eastern Nebraska, Iowa and then into Wisconsin before then moving east. This system spread moisture across central and northeast South Dakota, initially as a rain snow mix before transitioning to accumulating snowfall. Multi-day accumulations topped out at 9 inches in Milbank, 8.5 inches in La Bolt, 8 inches in Pickerel Lake, while Murdo came in at 6.1 inches. In addition to the snowfall, winds peaked between 40 and 50 mph with a peak gust of 53 mph at the Department of Transportation weather stations in Macs Corner and Lake City. The combination of high winds, snow and ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions, with multiple slide-ins and jackknifed semis along Interstate 29 and 90 as well as highways 12 and 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grant County Sheriff advised No Travel due to hazardous travel conditions.

A strong synoptic gradient ahead of a cold front resulted in sustained south to southwest winds from 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 45 to 55 mph with a couple locations up to around 60 mph. Accompanying low humidity values led to areas of critical fire behavior, but no significant fires were noted during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds reached a sustained 30 to 35 mph, with a peak gust up to 44 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KBKX in Brookings.

Accompanying low humidity values led to areas of critical fire behavior, but no significant fires were noted during the period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds reached a sustained 30 to 36 mph, with a peak gust up to 55 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KHON near Huron and 52 mph at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Wolsey.

Elsewhere across the northern Black Hills area and the west central South Dakota plains, amounts of 2 to 6 inches were reported in most areas.

Low pressure moving across South Dakota generated high winds, with peak gusts of 59 mph in Summit, and 58 mph at the Pierre Regional Airport as well as the DOT site at Craven Corner and in Reliance. Additionally, numerous weather stations across north central, central and northeast South Dakota recorded wind gusts of 50 mph or greater throughout the day.

An early spring storm system came through the region in several pieces, bringing a wide variety of weather to the area. Rain and snow developed initially with the first part of the system early on the 24th, with some freezing rain and a wintry mix across far southwest into south-central South Dakota. Snow was heaviest initially from the northern Black Hills area to northwest South Dakota, with heavier snow developing elsewhere to the southeast later on the 24th into the 25th. Gusty winds developed on the back side of the system across the western into central South Dakota plains, producing areas of blowing snow, with near blizzard conditions for a period on the 25th across far south-central South Dakota. The highest snowfall totals were generally from the Spearfish to Sturgis areas, and also across parts of northwest into central South Dakota, where 5 to 10 inches was mostly reported. Elsewhere across the northern Black Hills area and the west central South Dakota plains, amounts of 2 to 6 inches were reported in most areas.

An upper-level system moved across the region, bringing a couple of disturbances across the northern Plains. Accumulating snowfall with the first disturbance was mainly confined to the central and southern Black Hills on the 30th. A stronger disturbance moved across the area on the 31st into April 1st, and with colder air in place, produced a more widespread area of accumulating snowfall. Amounts of 2 to around 6 inches were common across much of western and south-central South Dakota, with at least localized higher amounts to around 10 inches from southwest into portions of south-central South Dakota.

Expansive high pressure out of Canada followed a clipper system resulting in high winds, arctic cold and dangerous wind chills on January 11th. A second system from the south followed close behind enhancing winds overnight January 12th into the 13th. Extreme cold with dangerous wind chills lingered through January 15th after which temperatures began to moderate. ||Record cold high temperatures were set at Pierre, with a high on January 12th of 1 degree below zero, and on January 13th with a high of only 9 degrees below zero. For Aberdeen, Sisseton and Watertown, the temperature remained below zero between the 13th through the 15th, while for Mobridge, the high temperature failed to exceed zero from the 12th through the 15th. ||The coldest temperatures were recorded in Eureka and 3 miles east-northeast of Gann Valley, with a low of 22 degrees below zero on the morning of the 15th, while Trail City, Ridgeview and McIntosh all had lows of 20 below. Most of the area witnessed a low of 15 below or colder on that morning as well. The main impact wasn't the cold however, it was the high winds that accompanied the cold which resulted in some extreme wind chill values. The morning of the 13th would see the most extreme of these wind chills, with 68 degrees below zero at Trail City and 67 degrees below zero at Ridgeview. 65 additional weather stations across north central, central and northeast portions of the state registered a wind chill between 45 and 65 below on that morning thanks to wind speeds anywhere between 45 and 55 mph. Additionally, wind chills would again dip below 45 degrees for much of north central and central South Dakota on the morning of the 15th with a minimum wind chill of 53 degrees below zero at both Trail City and Ridgeview thanks to winds of 45 to 55mph. ||The extreme cold was responsible for at least one exposure death.

Locally strong winds developed in the favored areas around the Sisseton hills during the overnight hours of Saturday, January 20th and the morning hours Sunday, January 21st. A peak wind gust of 65mph was observed at the DOT station 4 miles north of Altamont, while the DOT station in Summit gusted to 59mph. Additional wind gusts of 40 to 55mph were observed across much of northeast South Dakota.

Locally strong winds developed in the favored areas around the Sisseton hills during the overnight hours of Saturday, January 20th and the morning hours Sunday, January 21st. A peak wind gust of 65mph was observed at the DOT station 4 miles north of Altamont, while the DOT station in Summit gusted to 59mph. Additional wind gusts of 40 to 55mph were observed across much of northeast South Dakota.

A deep trough of low pressure pushed slowly out of the southern Rockies and spurred development of a strong area of low pressure, which moved slolwly from eastern Colorado on the 24th to the western Great Lakes on the 26th. Abundant warm air near the surface and aloft limited wintry impacts over northwest Iowa and forced a mix of wintry types or change to rain or drizzle as far west as the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota as surface temperatures climbed near or above freezing after the initial warm advection snow on the 24th.  This coated and crusted over the new snow cover and when intense winds which gusted to 40 to 55 mph developed on the 25th into the 26th behind the system, widespread blizzard conditions failed to develop. Nevertheless, hazardous travel conditions with numerous slide-ins and accidents were reported across much of southeast South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.||Multi-day snow accumulation was generally from 4 to 12 inches from south central to east central South Dakota and into northern areas of southwest Minnesota, with peak wind gusts as high as 55 mph near Wagner, Dallas, and Dixon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early but changed to a period of mixed precipitation by midday on the 24th as temperatures warmed above freezing. Snow redeveloped by early on the 25th, concurrent with north winds which gusted as high as 44 mph 5 miles southeast of Tyndall and 42 mph at Scotland to produce visibility as low as 1/2 mile at times.  Snow totaled 6.7 inches at Tyndall and 5.0 inches 8 miles west-northwest of Springfield.

Snow totaled 5.0 inches 5 miles southeast of Chamberlain and 4.7 inches near Pukwana.

Nevertheless, hazardous travel conditions with numerous slide-ins and accidents were reported across much of southeast South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.||Multi-day snow accumulation was generally from 4 to 12 inches from south central to east central South Dakota and into northern areas of southwest Minnesota, with peak wind gusts as high as 55 mph near Wagner, Dallas, and Dixon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early but changed to a period of mixed precipitation by midday on the 24th as temperatures warmed to near freezing. Snow redeveloped by late on the 24th, with a later increase in north winds which gusted as high as 43 mph 5 miles south of Arlington and 35 mph at Iroquois to produce visibility as low as 1/2 mile at times.  Snow totaled 4.7 inches at De Smet and 4.0 inches 9 miles south-southwest of Arlington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds were sustained at 30 to 35 mph, with a peak wind of 61 mph measured at the Automated Surface Observing System site KMHE 3 miles north of Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and accompanying cold front brought light snowfall to much of western South Dakota. Across portions of the Black Hills, amounts of 2 to 4 inches were noted, with locally higher amounts across the northern Black Hills and the Custer area.

A compact system crossing from west to east generated an approximately 100 mile wide band of snow across central and northeast South Dakota. Peak snowfall amounts include 7.9 inches 3 miles north northeast of Gann Valley,  7 inches 7 miles east of Hayes and in the city of Miller, 6.5 inches in both Hayti and 8 miles north of Vivian, and 6 inches in both Kennebec and 9 miles east of Grey Goose. Icy roads were responsible for several vehicle accidents including one direct fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Towing company posted photos of vehicles in the ditch as well as as a pair of semi trailers involved in an accident on Interstate 90.

A disturbance quickly passed across the central Plains, bringing some snow to southwest South Dakota. The heaviest snowfall developed across portions of Fall River County, where 2 to 4 inches of accumulation was reported in western portions of the county.

A quick moving disturbance brought accumulating snow to much of northwest and west central South Dakota during the late night and morning hours. The more significant snow developed from the northern Black Hills area to the west central South Dakota plains, where 3 to 5 inches generally fell, with the highest amounts in locally heavier bands from northeast Pennington and southeast Meade counties to Haakon County.

Above normal temperatures resulted in rapid snow melt across central South Dakota. This snow melt resulted in a rise in river levels of Medicine Creek which lasted for about a day before steadily receding. A peak crest of 1667.53 was observed on the morning of 2/9, with only minor rural flooding.

A compact system crossing from west to east generated an approximately 100 mile wide band of snow across central and northeast South Dakota. Peak snowfall amounts include 7.9 inches 3 miles north northeast of Gann Valley,  7 inches 7 miles east of Hayes and in the city of Miller, 6.5 inches in both Hayti and 8 miles north of Vivian, and 6 inches in both Kennebec and 9 miles east of Grey Goose. Icy roads were responsible for several vehicle accidents including one direct fatality.

Widespread thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours on May 23rd in the vicinity of a low pressure system and its associated warm and cold fronts. These storms produced large hail, damaging winds and a pair of funnel clouds. The largest hail observed was golf ball sized 9 miles west of Orient and 7 miles southeast of Burdette. 1 inch hail was also observed in the city of Aberdeen. In addition to the large hail, strong winds were also observed. A weather station 3 miles south of Saint Lawrence recording a wind gust of 76 mph, with an additional 4 other weather stations recording wind gusts in excess of 58 mph scattered across central, north central and northeast South Dakota.

A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across southeast South Dakota mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph. In a couple instances, these high winds were aided by nearby rain showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds briefly peaked between 40 and 43 mph in the morning hours and then more consistently peaked between 40 and 49 mph during the afternoon. Although sustained winds dropped below 40 mph into the evening, a maximum wind gust of 61 mph was observed shortly before midnight at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Cavour (SD 515), likely aided by nearby showers.

A line of severe thunderstorms developed across the west central South Dakota plains and tracked east-northeast into central South Dakota. The storms primarily produced wind gusts of up to 80 mph across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The spotter estimated wind gusts at near 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed along the eastern slopes of the Black Hills and tracked east-southeast across the west central South Dakota plains. The storm produced large hail to ping pong ball size and a funnel cloud near Kyle as the storm was weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground. The largest hail was ping pong ball size, with lots of penny to nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal elevated thunderstorms developed due a strong low-level jet and steep mid-level lapse rates, with some spotty large hail in far southeast South Dakota.  Farther north, storms weakened but developed a large stratiform region of rain, under which the strong synoptic flow was allowed to accelerate with areas of damaging wind gusts well away from storm updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wake low winds were periodically sustained around 40 mph and gusted over 60 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD527 near Forestburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wake low winds were sustained occasionally over 40 mph with gusts up to 66 mph at the Automated Surface Observing System site KHON in Huron and 73 mph at South Dakota Road Weather Information System site SD515 near Cavour.

Low pressure lifting into western Minnesota resulted in high winds with 25 weather stations in far eastern South Dakota and western Minnesota exceeding gusts of 45 mph. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was observed at the Department of Transportation weather station near Brandt. These strong winds also toppled a tree in the city of Watertown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A radio station shared photos of a tree that fell due to the winds in Watertown.

Weak low pressure moving through central South Dakota provided the focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Peak winds of 64 mph were measured at the Department of Transportation weather station 10 miles southwest of Chantier Creek Recreation Area.  Hail ranged from dime to ping pong sized with the largest of that falling 7 miles northwest of McLaughlin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph was responsible for knocking down Cottonwood tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving northwest to southeast helped support strong to severe storms that mainly generated high winds, with a peak wind gust of 90 mph at the Grand River Remote Automatic Weather Station (RAWS)  situated 11 miles south of the community of Bullhead. Additional wind gust resulted in damage to grain bins and power poles 2 miles north northwest of, and 3 miles northwest of Sand Lake Wildlife Refuge respectively. Shingle damage was reported at a residence 2 miles east of Eureka and thunderstorm winds also resulted in local brownout conditions 8 miles east northeast of Frederick.

While instability was somewhat limited from 1000-1500 J/kg MUCAPE, strong deep layer shear associated with a negatively tilted mid-level trough led to a low CAPE/high shear setup which favored discrete cellular development. The strongest updrafts produced marginally large hail during the afternoon hours, up to half-dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea to dime sized, but several were as large as a quarter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet April and early May, including heavy rain producing thunderstorms on May 6th, resulted in the North Fork Whetstone River briefly rising to above flood stage during the evening of May 7th into the morning of May 8th. The nearby Milbank COOP observer recorded 3.37 inches of rain in April and an additional 1.33 inches of rain during the first week of May. The river peaked at 1056.55 feet, or 0.55 feet above flood stage. These high river levels resulted in inundation of agricultural fields, and topped the low water crossing on 479th Ave.

Nocturnal elevated thunderstorms developed due a strong low-level jet and steep mid-level lapse rates, with some spotty large hail in far southeast South Dakota.  Farther north, storms weakened but developed a large stratiform region of rain, under which the strong synoptic flow was allowed to accelerate with areas of damaging wind gusts well away from storm updrafts.

A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball sized hail fell at the Sheriff's office. Hail was accompanied by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered mixed-mode thunderstorms developed along a cold front which moved through an environment of 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and up to 40 knots of deep-layer shear.  A few brief supercells developed before evolving to bowing structures and brought marginally large hail.

A cold front moving northwest to southeast helped support strong to severe storms that mainly generated high winds, with a peak wind gust of 90 mph at the Grand River Remote Automatic Weather Station (RAWS)  situated 11 miles south of the community of Bullhead. Additional wind gust resulted in damage to grain bins and power poles 2 miles north northwest of, and 3 miles northwest of Sand Lake Wildlife Refuge respectively. Shingle damage was reported at a residence 2 miles east of Eureka and thunderstorm winds also resulted in local brownout conditions 8 miles east northeast of Frederick.

The dissipating stratiform region from a large thunderstorm complex moved across southeast South Dakota in the early to mid-morning hours of May 21.  A wake low developed which produced a period of damaging wind gusts collocated with ending of rainfall while the feature lifted northeast across southeast South Dakota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 2 miles west-southwest of Pickstown measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

A strong spring storm system brough rain and strong winds across much of western South Dakota. Across the higher elevations of the northern Black Hills, mainly above 5500 feet, temperatures were cold enough for heavy, wet snow to accumulate. The highest elevations from Terry Peak to O'Neill Pass, and Rochford saw 6 to over 12 inches of accumulation, with near 18 inches at O'Neill Pass. Power outages were reported due to the heavy, wet snow and gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across southeast South Dakota.  Some limited moisture wrapped back southward around the area of low pressure and produced a narrow band of light to briefly moderate snowfall during the overnight and early morning hours of November 20 with a small area between 1 and 3.5 inches accumulation.  At times of falling snow, the very strong winds gusting from 40 to 65 mph produced some brief visibility less than one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained at times over 40 mph, with gusts measured as high as 68 mph 13 miles west of Platte and 58 mph near Academy and Wagner.

Heavy rainfall resulted in river flooding along the Big Sioux near Castlewood during he early morning hours of May 23rd. The river peaked at 9.22 feet above flood stage, and remained in flood through the afternoon. At that level the main impacts are flooding of low-lying agricultural lands, and water overtopping a culvert on 184th street.

The river crested at 9.1 feet before falling later that evening. At that level the main impacts are flooding of low-lying agricultural lands, and water overtopping a culvert on 184th street.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed in a warm air advection regime with 1500-2000 J/kg elevated MUCAPE and 35-40 knots of effective shear.  An isolated stronger cell developed on the western side of the cluster and produced damaging wind and large hail.

The dissipating stratiform region from a large thunderstorm complex moved across southeast South Dakota in the early to mid-morning hours of May 21.  A wake low developed which produced a period of damaging wind gusts collocated with ending of rainfall while the feature lifted northeast across southeast South Dakota.  Wind gusts were measured frequently from 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 43 mph with a peak wind gust of 61 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 4 miles west of Plankinton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was in a very rural area, with no damage. Therefore, a damage assessment could not be conducted. The tornado was small and thin, traveling approximately 10 miles across open prairie. Tornado track is based on radar and spotter reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across southeast South Dakota mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph. In a couple instances, these high winds were aided by nearby rain showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds intermittently peaked between 40 and 42 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 60 mph was observed at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Irene (SD 615).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across southeast South Dakota mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph. In a couple instances, these high winds were aided by nearby rain showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds very briefly peaked near 40 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the Mitchell Municipal Airport (KMHE).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6.  While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts measured by mobile mesonet.  Winds were accompanied by heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A considerable amount of nickel to quarter size hail fell.

An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across southeast South Dakota.  Some limited moisture wrapped back southward around the area of low pressure and produced a narrow band of light to briefly moderate snowfall during the overnight and early morning hours of November 20 with a small area between 1 and 3.5 inches accumulation.  At times of falling snow, the very strong winds gusting from 40 to 65 mph produced some brief visibility less than one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief wind gust of 59 mph was measured at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very light snowfall less than one inch occurred with warm ground temperatures right before the evening rush hour to create significant travel headaches in the pre-Thanksgiving timeframe.  Melt and refreeze resulted in over 50 automobile accidents around the Sioux Falls metro area alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite only from one-quarter to three-quarters of an inch snowfall around the Sioux Falls metro area, the above freezing ground temperatures led to a melt and refreeze of the snowfall with heavy pre-holiday evening rush-hour traffic.  Roads quickly became treacherous with a glaze of ice with temperatures slipping through the 20s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated early morning thunderstorms brought hail to the size of quarters and strong winds to portions of central and north central South Dakota. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was observed by the DOT weather station 5 miles west northwest of Mclaughlin, and wind driven hail was responsible for damaging siding to a residence 3 miles north northeast of Iona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail to the size of nickels and an estimated 60 mph thunderstorm winds was responsible for damage to the siding on their home.

Slow moving low pressure generated a pocket of prolonged heavy rain over a portion of northeast South Dakota.  Radar estimates of rainfall ranged between 2 and 5 inches in a broad area covering portions of Brown, Day, Clark and Codington counties, with public measurements of between 2.76 and 3.5 inches on the west side of Watertown, and an observed rainfall of 2.85 inches near Ferney. Portions of the Stokes-Thomas Lake City Park campground at Lake Kampeska experienced flooding as a result of this heavy rain. A trained spotter also reported water running over several roads 3 miles north of Bradley in Clark county. This heavy rain was also responsible for minor flooding along Mud Creek and the South Branch Tributary of Mud Creek both near Rauville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Campers reported flooding in portions of the Stokes-Thomas Lake City Park campground. Photos submitted included deep standing water with partially inundated campers and tents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front supported the generation of storms that were occasionally severe with hail and gusty winds. Golfball hail was observed 4 miles north of Orient while several other locations observed hail to the size of quarters. Wind damage was also observed, including tree branches down in Frankfort and 4 miles south southwest of Crocker.  The weather station in Redfield also observed a wind gust of 61 mph, which resulted in downed powerlines and tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across central South Dakota during the late evening and early overnight hours. The storms mainly generated strong winds, with observed gusts as high as 74 mph. These thunderstorm winds were responsible for damaging a pole barn located 1 mile south of Highmore. Additionally, a farmer later indicated that a tornado had touched down in an open field, which was confirmed by satellite data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region witnessed three days of high heat and humidity in late July. Peak temperatures for the three day period included a high of 105 degrees measured at the South Dakota extension mesonet station 7 miles southwest of Hayes, 104 degrees at Pierre municipal airport and 103 degrees at the department of transportation weather station in Hayes. The South Dakota extension mesonet station 7 miles southwest of Hayes also observed a high temperature in excess of 100 degrees for all 3 days. ||In addition to the heat, high humidity with dewpoints in the low to mid 70s resulted in widespread heat index values at or above 100 degrees.  A public weather station in Pierre observed a heat index of 108 degrees on July 27th, with Pierre municipal airport measuring a peak heat index of 107 degrees on July 25th. 29 weather stations recorded a peak heat index of 100 degrees or higher on July 25th, 18 on the 26th and a total of 25 stations observed a heat index of 100 degrees or more on the 27th.

A large storm trekking across western into southern portions of the state crossed through Jones county with up to tennis-ball sized hail and damaging winds. The Jones county sheriff reported several hail damaged vehicles on Interstate 90, and that strong winds had flipped over some campers and knocked over motorcyclists. A CoCoRaHS observer 12 miles west of Murdo measured the hail at 2.5 inches. While other storms were in the area, few other reports of severe weather occurred with these storms, with the most notable being the weather station 2 miles north of Harreid (Campbell county) recording a wind gust of 70 mph. USDA crop indemnities for Jones county topped 188 thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported that their vehicles windows had been shattered and the homes roof shingles had been damaged by tennis-ball sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across central South Dakota during the late evening and early overnight hours. The storms mainly generated strong winds, with observed gusts as high as 74 mph. These thunderstorm winds were responsible for damaging a pole barn located 1 mile south of Highmore. Additionally, a farmer later indicated that a tornado had touched down in an open field, which was confirmed by satellite data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reports broken tree branches and power knocked out.

A large storm trekking across western into southern portions of the state crossed through Jones county with up to tennis-ball sized hail and damaging winds. The Jones county sheriff reported several hail damaged vehicles on Interstate 90, and that strong winds had flipped over some campers and knocked over motorcyclists. A CoCoRaHS observer 12 miles west of Murdo measured the hail at 2.5 inches. While other storms were in the area, few other reports of severe weather occurred with these storms, with the most notable being the weather station 2 miles north of Harreid (Campbell county) recording a wind gust of 70 mph. USDA crop indemnities for Jones county topped 188 thousand dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jones county sheriff reported that campers and motorcyclists were blown over while traveling down Interstate 90. Hail had also damaged several vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm temperatures with highs in the lower to mid 90s paired with oppressive dew point readings in the upper 70s to lower 80s produced afternoon and evening heat indices generally from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread oppressive conditions occurred during the afternoon and evening on July 13, with a secondary peak of heat across far southeast South Dakota on July 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 105 degrees with a maximum heat index of 105 degrees at the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Alexandria on July 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Marginal mixed-layer instability and effective bulk shear around 30 knots led to weak organization of scattered storms during the late afternoon and evening hours from eastern South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. The strongest cells produced up to quarter size hail. A deeper sub-cloud dry layer supported spotty stronger wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage monetary losses are an estimate using data from the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient and elevated winds atop a well mixed boundary layer ahead of a low pressure system across Nebraska resulted in high winds across southeast South Dakota mainly during the afternoon and evening hours of April 6th. Multiple automated stations reported maximum wind gusts at or above 58 mph. In a couple instances, these high winds were aided by nearby rain showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds infrequently peaked between 40 and 43 mph during the afternoon hours. A maximum wind gust of 60 mph was observed at Sioux Falls Regional Airport (KFSD).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of thunderstorms developed across western South Dakota during the evening hours of July 13 and spread eastward through the night. The environment featured 2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 25 to 35 knots of deep-layer shear, and an increasingly warm and dry capped layer farther eastward through South Dakota. Widespread damaging wind developed with the mesoscale convective system with gusts from 60 to 75 mph common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended duration of strong to severe winds gusted at times to 61 mph at the SDSU mesonet station 16 miles north of Gregory.

Widespread damaging wind developed with the mesoscale convective system with gusts from 60 to 75 mph common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph was measured by the South Dakota Road Weather Information System site near Corsica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cluster of thunderstorms resided near Yankton for a couple hours, with a deep warm cloud layer resulting in extremely efficient rainfall rates of up to 3-4 inches per hour at times.  Rain totals from 3 to 6 inches were measured around the Yankton area over just a couple hours. Water quickly pooled and flowed up to a couple feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant urban flooding occurred at the entrance to Mount Marty College with water standing up to several feet and water flowing over the driveway to just under a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a weak boundary during the evening with up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and increasing deep-layer shear as a mid-level wind maxima approached from western Nebraska and southwest South Dakota. The strongest storm produced up to baseball size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from golf ball to baseball size. Multiple windshields were broken with large dents in vehicles and corn was stripped of most of its leaves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels from the historic June flooding remained above flood stage for some locations into July, although continued a declining trend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from June on the James River near Mitchell, with declining river levels. The river fell below flood stage on the evening of July 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed over southeast Montana and merged into a line of severe thunderstorms as the moved across far southeast Montana and into northwest South Dakota. The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 50 to over 80 mph, with some areas within the line producing wind gusts of 80 to over 100 mph. Considerable damage was noted with the stronger wind gusts across portions of northwest South Dakota, especially southern Harding, northern Butte, northern and eastern Meade, and Haakon counties.  The line of storms was classified as a derecho event as it tracked more than 250 miles across the region from southeast Montana to central South Dakota.

A line of strong thunderstorms moved across north central and northeast South Dakota during the morning of July 31st. Wind damage started near Britton, with a tower blown down at the ballpark and several grain bins knocked over. Thunderstorm winds continued east flipping pontoon boats near Lake City. A peak wind gust of 92 mph was measured at the Hillhead Remote Automatic Weather Station around the same time that a roof was partly blow off of a dairy barn near Veblen. That complex of storms continued east into Minnesota during the early afternoon, with additional thunderstorms developing across northeast South Dakota during the afternoon. Those storms produced hail to the size of half-dollars 3 miles north of Sisseton and sporadic tree damage in Codington and Hamlin counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6.  While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road was blocked by a large tree branch taken down by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front supported the generation of storms that were occasionally severe with hail and gusty winds. Golfball hail was observed 4 miles north of Orient while several other locations observed hail to the size of quarters. Wind damage was also observed, including tree branches down in Frankfort and 4 miles south southwest of Crocker.  The weather station in Redfield also observed a wind gust of 61 mph, which resulted in downed powerlines and tree damage.

A line of storms produced high winds and wind damage across a good portion of central South Dakota during the late evening Saturday July 13th into the early morning hours Sunday July 14th. This line of storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 109 mph 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc, along with sporadic damage prior to reaching the cities of Pierre and Fort Pierre. In and around the Pierre area, there were numerous reports of toppling of trees or limbs coming down in addition to damage to several buildings, outbuildings, campers and signage. The peak wind gust recorded by the weather station at Pierre regional airport was 84 mph. 12 weather stations spread across 10 counties recorded peak winds in excess of 70 mph. Power infrastructure was also impacted by this system with 412 customers reported to have lost power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported thunderstorm wind damage in the form of 3 inch diameter limbs broken off trees in addition to several power poles that had also snapped.

The weather station in Redfield also observed a wind gust of 61 mph, which resulted in downed powerlines and tree damage.

A line of storms produced high winds and wind damage across a good portion of central South Dakota during the late evening Saturday July 13th into the early morning hours Sunday July 14th. This line of storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 109 mph 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc, along with sporadic damage prior to reaching the cities of Pierre and Fort Pierre. In and around the Pierre area, there were numerous reports of toppling of trees or limbs coming down in addition to damage to several buildings, outbuildings, campers and signage. The peak wind gust recorded by the weather station at Pierre regional airport was 84 mph. 12 weather stations spread across 10 counties recorded peak winds in excess of 70 mph. Power infrastructure was also impacted by this system with 412 customers reported to have lost power.

A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6.  While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A livestock shelter was blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches up to several inches in diameter were taken down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed over southeast Montana and merged into a line of severe thunderstorms as the moved across far southeast Montana and into northwest South Dakota. The storms produced widespread wind gusts of 50 to over 80 mph, with some areas within the line producing wind gusts of 80 to over 100 mph. Considerable damage was noted with the stronger wind gusts across portions of northwest South Dakota, especially southern Harding, northern Butte, northern and eastern Meade, and Haakon counties.  The line of storms was classified as a derecho event as it tracked more than 250 miles across the region from southeast Montana to central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts of 60 to around 100 mph for at least 15 minutes.

A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows were broken and portions of the roof were removed from a single family home. | The thunderstorm winds also downed numerous large tree limbs.

The dissipating stratiform region from a large thunderstorm complex moved across southeast South Dakota in the early to mid-morning hours of May 21.  A wake low developed which produced a period of damaging wind gusts collocated with ending of rainfall while the feature lifted northeast across southeast South Dakota.  Wind gusts were measured frequently from 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 59 mph were measured by a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 3 miles north of Dolton.  A pickup and 40 foot travel trailer was blown off of Interstate 90 just south of Salem at 0808 CST, and several small limbs were downed in Salem at 0810CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dissipating stratiform region from a large thunderstorm complex moved across southeast South Dakota in the early to mid-morning hours of May 21.  A wake low developed which produced a period of damaging wind gusts collocated with ending of rainfall while the feature lifted northeast across southeast South Dakota.  Wind gusts were measured frequently from 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous sites around Sioux Falls measured wind gusts from 58 to 63 mph shortly after 0800 CST resulting in several large limbs down and spotty brief power outages.  A semi-trailer was blown off Interstate 90 just north of Brandon and resulted in an indirect injury to the driver of the semi truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced pockets of heavy rainfall and very efficient rainfall rates. This resulted in localized flash flooding reports across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. Even after the heaviest rainfall rates had ended, a stripe of prolonged moderate rainfall resulted in excessive ponding and some minor flooding of rural roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm produced a rapid accumulation of 1.5 to 3 inches of rain in less than an hours time. This resulted in flash flooding and water flowing over at least a couple county roads, including along 476th between 285th and 287th. The water pushed vegetation debris onto the road, which required the use of snowplows for removal.

A line of storms produced high winds and wind damage across a good portion of central South Dakota during the late evening Saturday July 13th into the early morning hours Sunday July 14th. This line of storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 109 mph 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc, along with sporadic damage prior to reaching the cities of Pierre and Fort Pierre. In and around the Pierre area, there were numerous reports of toppling of trees or limbs coming down in addition to damage to several buildings, outbuildings, campers and signage. The peak wind gust recorded by the weather station at Pierre regional airport was 84 mph. 12 weather stations spread across 10 counties recorded peak winds in excess of 70 mph. Power infrastructure was also impacted by this system with 412 customers reported to have lost power.

Slow moving low pressure generated a pocket of prolonged heavy rain over a portion of northeast South Dakota.  Radar estimates of rainfall ranged between 2 and 5 inches in a broad area covering portions of Brown, Day, Clark and Codington counties, with public measurements of between 2.76 and 3.5 inches on the west side of Watertown, and an observed rainfall of 2.85 inches near Ferney. Portions of the Stokes-Thomas Lake City Park campground at Lake Kampeska experienced flooding as a result of this heavy rain. A trained spotter also reported water running over several roads 3 miles north of Bradley in Clark county. This heavy rain was also responsible for minor flooding along Mud Creek and the South Branch Tributary of Mud Creek both near Rauville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud Creek near Rauville rose above flood stage on July 5th at 1145am CST, cresting at 9.30 feet, or 0.3 feet above flood stage on July 5th at 5pm, before slowly falling below flood stage on July 6th at 330am CST.

Considerable damage was noted with the stronger wind gusts across portions of northwest South Dakota, especially southern Harding, northern Butte, northern and eastern Meade, and Haakon counties.  The line of storms was classified as a derecho event as it tracked more than 250 miles across the region from southeast Montana to central South Dakota.

A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6.  While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hoop house was blown over and multiple large branches were taken down by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms produced high winds and wind damage across a good portion of central South Dakota during the late evening Saturday July 13th into the early morning hours Sunday July 14th. This line of storms produced wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 109 mph 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc, along with sporadic damage prior to reaching the cities of Pierre and Fort Pierre. In and around the Pierre area, there were numerous reports of toppling of trees or limbs coming down in addition to damage to several buildings, outbuildings, campers and signage. The peak wind gust recorded by the weather station at Pierre regional airport was 84 mph. 12 weather stations spread across 10 counties recorded peak winds in excess of 70 mph. Power infrastructure was also impacted by this system with 412 customers reported to have lost power.

A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph. In the wake of the evening convection, scattered elevated storms developed, which produced a combination of damaging winds and spotty large hail during the very early morning hours of July 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable tree damage was reported in Springfield due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed just west of Rapid City, clipping western and southern portions of Rapid City, then tracked onto the plains before weakening over southeast Pennington County. The storm produced hail to over quarter size.

A large supercell thunderstorm developed over western Jackson County and tracked southeast through Bennett County and into Nebraska. The storm caused considerable damage in Potato Creek and the Martin area with very large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home was rolled over.

The first widespread heavier snowfall of the season occurred over a two-day period, resulting in 8 to 14 inches of accumulation. The first round of snow began to spread northward across the area very early on January 8 as some areas featured rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the morning and midday across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and western portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.  After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow. ||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th.  Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 8.8 inches in Yankton.

A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -53 degrees near Erwin and -52 degrees near Arlington and Iroquois during the late afternoon and early evening on January 13.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -56 degrees near Plankinton and -54 degrees at White Lake around midday on January 13.

A very moist and unstable atmosphere was present in the wake of earlier day convection, as another stronger mid-level wave and associated frontal boundary pushed through southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening and early overnight hours. Initial development of an isolated supercell in south central South Dakota resulted in hail up to 2 inches in diameter, but more widespread convection along the boundary later in the evening led to areas of damaging wind and excessive rainfall in east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Rainfall was heaviest near the Brookings area with several gauge amounts of 4 to 8 inches around the city.||Indirect fatalities occurred when increasing wave action on an area lake associated with thunderstorm winds resulted in the drowning of two individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water was flowing over the curb along 6th Street/Highway 14 near the movie theater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread heavier snowfall of the season occurred over a two-day period, resulting in 8 to 14 inches of accumulation. The first round of snow began to spread northward across the area very early on January 8 as some areas featured rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the morning and midday across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and western portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.  After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow. ||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th.  Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches in Madison.

A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph. In the wake of the evening convection, scattered elevated storms developed, which produced a combination of damaging winds and spotty large hail during the very early morning hours of July 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with hail up to the size of nickels, wind gusts to around 50 mph resulted in stripped leaves from trees.

Low pressure over south central Canada and high pressure nudging into western South Dakota generated strong wind gusts across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on both June 5th and June 6th. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Summit Department of Transportation weather station, with gusts of 59 mph and 58 mph observed with mesonet weather stations run by the Department of Transportation near Foster Bay and 4 miles north of Altamont respectively. Additional wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were recorded on an additional 53 weather stations across the forecast area. These strong winds and locally dry conditions resulted in spotty areas of blowing dust.

A line of storms forming along a front produced sporadic high winds in portions of north central and northeast South Dakota during the overnight hours of July 17th into the early morning hours of July 18th. Wind speeds of 81 mph were recorded 4 miles north of Agar, 70 mph 1 mile east-southeast of Bowdle and 61 mph 5 miles west of Seneca and at the Aberdeen regional airport. The COOP observer 1 mile north of Pollock also estimated wind speeds topped out at 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were observed in the south portion of Aberdeen, with a brief power outage in the southwest portion of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -49 degrees near Tyndall and Scotland during the afternoon of January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief pulse of mid-level warm advection enhanced an area of light snow which moved across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. In just a few hours, snowfall as much as 3 to 5 inches fell in a fairly small area from just west of Sioux Falls southeast to Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.5 inches 7 miles north of Parker, and 2.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose.

As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -55 degrees near Lane and -53 degrees at Wessington Springs during midday on January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread heavier snowfall of the season occurred over a two-day period, resulting in 8 to 14 inches of accumulation. The first round of snow began to spread northward across the area very early on January 8 as some areas featured rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the morning and midday across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and western portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.  After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow. ||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th.  Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totaled 5.6 inches at Huron and 4.5 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere was present in the wake of earlier day convection, as another stronger mid-level wave and associated frontal boundary pushed through southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening and early overnight hours. Initial development of an isolated supercell in south central South Dakota resulted in hail up to 2 inches in diameter, but more widespread convection along the boundary later in the evening led to areas of damaging wind and excessive rainfall in east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota. Rainfall was heaviest near the Brookings area with several gauge amounts of 4 to 8 inches around the city.||Indirect fatalities occurred when increasing wave action on an area lake associated with thunderstorm winds resulted in the drowning of two individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several brief funnel clouds were recorded on video northwest of Scotland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief pulse of mid-level warm advection enhanced an area of light snow which moved across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. In just a few hours, snowfall as much as 3 to 5 inches fell in a fairly small area from just west of Sioux Falls southeast to Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches near Davis and 4.0 inches at Chancellor.

The storms produced mostly small hail and wind gusts to over 70 mph across west central into south central South Dakota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over south central Canada and high pressure nudging into western South Dakota generated strong wind gusts across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on both June 5th and June 6th. A peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Summit Department of Transportation weather station, with gusts of 59 mph and 58 mph observed with mesonet weather stations run by the Department of Transportation near Foster Bay and 4 miles north of Altamont respectively. Additional wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were recorded on an additional 53 weather stations across the forecast area. These strong winds and locally dry conditions resulted in spotty areas of blowing dust.

An isolated thunderstorm developed in far eastern South Dakota on the afternoon of June 18th, moving rapidly northeast, and peaking in intensity as it moved over the city of Watertown. Hail to the size of golfballs was observed 4 miles southeast of Rauville.

A line of storms forming along a front produced sporadic high winds in portions of north central and northeast South Dakota during the overnight hours of July 17th into the early morning hours of July 18th. Wind speeds of 81 mph were recorded 4 miles north of Agar, 70 mph 1 mile east-southeast of Bowdle and 61 mph 5 miles west of Seneca and at the Aberdeen regional airport. The COOP observer 1 mile north of Pollock also estimated wind speeds topped out at 60 mph.

The COOP observer 1 mile north of Pollock also estimated wind speeds topped out at 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer estimated the thunderstorm wind gusts topped out at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph. In the wake of the evening convection, scattered elevated storms developed, which produced a combination of damaging winds and spotty large hail during the very early morning hours of July 30.

Rainfall was heaviest near the Brookings area with several gauge amounts of 4 to 8 inches around the city.||Indirect fatalities occurred when increasing wave action on an area lake associated with thunderstorm winds resulted in the drowning of two individuals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff from intense rainfall led to multiple feet of standing water under the 6th Avenue South railroad bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -52 degrees near Alexandria around midday on January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A progressive cluster of thunderstorms developed across south central South Dakota in response to a weak mid-level wave drifting out of western South Dakota. A slight increase in the low-level jet brought deep-layer shear to 35-40 knots, and MUCAPE around 2000 J/kg provided fuel for storms to strengthen and congeal into a bowing structure which traversed to the east-southeast. The strongest storms produced damaging winds generally from 60 to 80 mph. In the wake of the evening convection, scattered elevated storms developed, which produced a combination of damaging winds and spotty large hail during the very early morning hours of July 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts measured on a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northern Perkins County and moved over far northeastern portions of the county. The storm produced large hail to around golf ball size from the Shadehill area to south of Lemmon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail caused damage to vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first widespread heavier snowfall of the season occurred over a two-day period, resulting in 8 to 14 inches of accumulation. The first round of snow began to spread northward across the area very early on January 8 as some areas featured rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour during the morning and midday across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and western portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.  After a brief break, another band of snow developed to the south and wrapped back into northwest Iowa and adjacent areas during the late afternoon and evening hours, again with brief periods of heavier snowfall.||The snow was accompanied by wind gusts of up to 25 to 35 mph, which resulted in some blowing and considerable drifting snow. ||Numerous locations set daily snowfall records on either or both January 8th and 9th.  Sioux Falls, SD and Sioux City IA set a daily record on January 8, and snowfall over the two-day period ranked in the top 10 for the month of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 10.0 inches 5 miles west of Armour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong storms during the morning hours of June 12th produced mainly hail and strong winds across portions of northeast South Dakota. The strongest storms generated hail between the size of dimes to golfballs in and around the Watertown area. Broken windows were reported in and around Lake Kampeska.

Elevated river levels out of the Niobrara River in north central Nebraska from seasonal freeze-up and silt deposit resulted in river levels at a few gages upstream of Gavins Point Dam pushing toward or above Action Stage. Impacts during the cold season from Minor Flooding are limited to seasonal cabins and recreation roads and thus cause minimal issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river rose above flood stage on the morning of the 11th before cresting 0.89 ft above flood stage (10.89 ft) on the morning of the 12th. The river then briefly dipped below flood stage before rising again and finally falling below flood stage for good on the 19th. Impacts during the cold season are limited to seasonal cabins and recreation roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing from west to east across the state provided the support for numerous, widespread severe thunderstorms. The main threat from these storms was large hail, with several reports of hail at or greater than golfball size. Emergency management for Brown county observed hail from golfball to baseball size 3 miles north northwest of Barnard, and hail to the size of baseballs was observed at multiple locations in Hand County. Peak thunderstorm wind gusts measured 65 mph 4 miles west of Waubay, and thunderstorm winds were responsible for knocking over some campers along Lake Poinsett. In addition to the large hail and winds, heavy rain produced overland flooding, with water reported over the roads in Hand, Hamlin, Codington and Clark counties. Observed 24 hour rainfall totals measured 6.25 inches 2 miles north of Willow Lake (Clark county), 3.84 inches 7 miles southwest of Estelline (Hamlin county), 2.4 inches 4 miles west of Hayti (Hamlin county) and 2.4 inches 2 miles west of Rauville (Codington county) . This heavy rain then translated into river flooding on June 3rd.

A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall.  Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.5 inches near Tea, 2.9 inches at Canton, and 2.5 inches near Harrisburg. Visibility was frequently around 1/2 mile at times of falling snow during the late morning and early afternoon.

Observed 24 hour rainfall totals measured 6.25 inches 2 miles north of Willow Lake (Clark county), 3.84 inches 7 miles southwest of Estelline (Hamlin county), 2.4 inches 4 miles west of Hayti (Hamlin county) and 2.4 inches 2 miles west of Rauville (Codington county) . This heavy rain then translated into river flooding on June 3rd.

A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall.  Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Bridgewater and 3.0 inches at 6 miles northwest of Montrose, 4 miles east-northeast of Monroe, and 5 miles northeast of Salem. Visibility was as low as 1/2 mile at times of falling snow in the late morning and early afternoon hours.

Low pressure crossing from west to east across the state provided the support for numerous, widespread severe thunderstorms. The main threat from these storms was large hail, with several reports of hail at or greater than golfball size. Emergency management for Brown county observed hail from golfball to baseball size 3 miles north northwest of Barnard, and hail to the size of baseballs was observed at multiple locations in Hand County. Peak thunderstorm wind gusts measured 65 mph 4 miles west of Waubay, and thunderstorm winds were responsible for knocking over some campers along Lake Poinsett. In addition to the large hail and winds, heavy rain produced overland flooding, with water reported over the roads in Hand, Hamlin, Codington and Clark counties. Observed 24 hour rainfall totals measured 6.25 inches 2 miles north of Willow Lake (Clark county), 3.84 inches 7 miles southwest of Estelline (Hamlin county), 2.4 inches 4 miles west of Hayti (Hamlin county) and 2.4 inches 2 miles west of Rauville (Codington county) . This heavy rain then translated into river flooding on June 3rd.

A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches with pockets in excess of 15 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating several towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Aurora, Bon Homme, Brule, Charles Mix, Clay, Davison, Douglas, Gregory, Hanson, Hutchinson, Lake, Lincoln, McCook, Miner, Minnehaha, Moody, Sanborn, Turner, Union, and Yankton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Vermillion River near Chancellor rose above flood stage the morning of June 21st, cresting around 7.87 feet above flood stage (1297.87 feet) on June 22nd.  This crest is in the Major Stage. The river then fell below flood stage on the evening of June 27th. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Turner County.
