A cluster of thunderstorms developed in a warm air advection regime with 1500-2000 J/kg elevated MUCAPE and 35-40 knots of effective shear.  An isolated stronger cell developed on the western side of the cluster and produced damaging wind and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in Salem and tipped over a semi-truck near town which resulted in minor direct injuries.

Persistent rain across portions of Codington and Hamlin counties resulted in flooding along the South Branch Tributary of Mud Creek, along with a brief rise above flood stage for the Big Sioux near Castlewood. Rainfall measured by the Watertown and Castlewood COOPs topped out at 1.35 and 1.36 inches respectively. The South Branch Tributary of Mud Creek entered flood stage on June 22nd at 7pm CST and remained above flood until 315pm CST on June 23rd, with a peak of 4.66 feet, though no impacts were noted.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over southwest Perkins County and tracked southeast across northeast Meade County and into southern Ziebach County, before weakening further to the east. The storm produced very large hail to near baseball size and gusty winds. An EF-2 tornado developed near highway 212, about 17 miles west of Faith, and moved southeast across mostly open prairie. Some power poles and trees were snapped along its path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed about 17 miles west of Faith, just north of Highway 212. As the tornado moved southeast and crossed Highway 212, numerous power poles were downed and broken. Some trees were also snapped off near the trunk and large branches broken off of other trees in this area. Additional trees were downed further southeast, before the tornado dissipated. The tornado did not impact any buildings or property along its path. The tornado traveled about 4 miles and was rated an EF-2, with wind speeds as high as 118 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -52 degrees at Chamberlain and Kimball during midday on January 13.

Thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall with between 2 to 4 inches of moisture on June 2nd were responsible for the flooding along tributaries and portions of the main stem of the Big Sioux river. Between June 3rd through June 6th the Big Sioux near Castlewood was in flood, with a peak of 10.34 feet at 9pm CST on June 4th, which mainly impacted agricultural land and low lying areas, although flooding did impact portions of 184th street. The Big Sioux river near Florence crested at 8.02 feet on June 3rd at 11am CST with flooding limited to agricultural areas. Willow Creek near Watertown topped out at 11.65 feet on June 3rd at 10am CST, though no impacts were noted.

Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced flooding across a broad swath of Lyman, Buffalo and Hand counties. In Lyman county, 7.46 inches was observed at a weather station 6 miles south southwest of Oacoma, while the South Dakota State University mesonet station near Oacoma recorded 5.51 inches. A CoCoRaHS observer 5 miles west southwest of Oacoma also observed 4.79 inches. In nearby Buffalo county, 3.56 inches of rain was measured 4 miles east northeast of Shelby. Damage with this round of flooding was assessed at 133,987 dollars in Buffalo county and 191,004 dollars in Hand county. Aside from overland flooding, heavy rain resulted in rises along the White River leading to flooding of agricultural lands on the southern border of Lyman county. This flooding was part of a broader event that resulted in a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which covered Hand and Buffalo counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported several county roads under as much as 3 to 4 feet of water. Additionally, runoff from an estimated three to five inches of rain caused the White River near Oacoma to crest at 16.07 ft at 215am CST. Minor flooding of agricultural land was observed in association with this river flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall produced flooding across a broad swath of Lyman, Buffalo and Hand counties. In Lyman county, 7.46 inches was observed at a weather station 6 miles south southwest of Oacoma, while the South Dakota State University mesonet station near Oacoma recorded 5.51 inches. A CoCoRaHS observer 5 miles west southwest of Oacoma also observed 4.79 inches. In nearby Buffalo county, 3.56 inches of rain was measured 4 miles east northeast of Shelby. Damage with this round of flooding was assessed at 133,987 dollars in Buffalo county and 191,004 dollars in Hand county. Aside from overland flooding, heavy rain resulted in rises along the White River leading to flooding of agricultural lands on the southern border of Lyman county. This flooding was part of a broader event that resulted in a Presidential Disaster Declaration, which covered Hand and Buffalo counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported water over several roadways in portions of Buffalo county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -52 degrees at Ethan and -47 degrees at Mitchell around midday on January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 49 mph at Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed quickly over the Silver City area and tracked northeast over the Summerset area, then east-northeast across the southern Meade County plains. Very large hail to over baseball size fell in the Summerset area, causing considerable property damage. Hail diminished some with the storm across the Meade County plains, but winds increased to near 60 mph, before the storm dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to over baseball size fell for 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing from west to east across the state provided the support for numerous, widespread severe thunderstorms. The main threat from these storms was large hail, with several reports of hail at or greater than golfball size. Emergency management for Brown county observed hail from golfball to baseball size 3 miles north northwest of Barnard, and hail to the size of baseballs was observed at multiple locations in Hand County. Peak thunderstorm wind gusts measured 65 mph 4 miles west of Waubay, and thunderstorm winds were responsible for knocking over some campers along Lake Poinsett. In addition to the large hail and winds, heavy rain produced overland flooding, with water reported over the roads in Hand, Hamlin, Codington and Clark counties. Observed 24 hour rainfall totals measured 6.25 inches 2 miles north of Willow Lake (Clark county), 3.84 inches 7 miles southwest of Estelline (Hamlin county), 2.4 inches 4 miles west of Hayti (Hamlin county) and 2.4 inches 2 miles west of Rauville (Codington county) . This heavy rain then translated into river flooding on June 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall.  Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at Howard. Visibility was as low as 1/2 mile at times of falling snow and wind gusts up to around 30 mph in the late morning and early afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.5 to 4.0 inches around the Sioux Falls metro area and 3.0 inches at Colton.  Visibility was frequently around 1/2 mile at times of falling snow during the late morning and early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient, combined with a strong low-level jet resulted in high winds across portions of southeast South Dakota on January 21. Isolated gusts above 58 mph were recorded in Gregory County. These winds resulted in some blowing and drifting snow with reduced visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated sustained winds from 40 to 52 mph persisted during the early morning hours of January 21. A peak wind gust of 65 mph was recorded at the South Dakota Department of Transportation site near Dixon.

Emergency management for Brown county observed hail from golfball to baseball size 3 miles north northwest of Barnard, and hail to the size of baseballs was observed at multiple locations in Hand County. Peak thunderstorm wind gusts measured 65 mph 4 miles west of Waubay, and thunderstorm winds were responsible for knocking over some campers along Lake Poinsett. In addition to the large hail and winds, heavy rain produced overland flooding, with water reported over the roads in Hand, Hamlin, Codington and Clark counties. Observed 24 hour rainfall totals measured 6.25 inches 2 miles north of Willow Lake (Clark county), 3.84 inches 7 miles southwest of Estelline (Hamlin county), 2.4 inches 4 miles west of Hayti (Hamlin county) and 2.4 inches 2 miles west of Rauville (Codington county) . This heavy rain then translated into river flooding on June 3rd.

A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.1 inches at Tyndall.  Winds gusting to 30 to 35 mph resulted in several hours of visibility less than one mile in the morning of January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches with pockets in excess of 15 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating several towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Aurora, Bon Homme, Brule, Charles Mix, Clay, Davison, Douglas, Gregory, Hanson, Hutchinson, Lake, Lincoln, McCook, Miner, Minnehaha, Moody, Sanborn, Turner, Union, and Yankton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls Western Ave rose above flood stage the morning of June 21st, cresting around 7.44 feet above flood stage (15.44 feet) on June 22nd.  This crest is in the Major Stage and caused significant flooding to parks along the river. Several roads near the river in Sioux Falls were also flooded for a period of time. The river then fell below flood stage on the afternoon of June 24th. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Minnehaha County.

A supercell thunderstorm developed over eastern Pennington County and tracked east-southeast into Haakon County, producing large hail and wind gusts to over 60 mph. A brief funnel cloud was reported northwest of Philip as well.

A deep low pressure to the south resulted in strong easterly winds across central, north central and northeast South Dakota late Friday night through the early morning hours of Sunday, with the bulk of the high winds being recorded on Saturday, April 6th. These strong winds were responsible for at least one small fire in Spink county, a vehicle accident on Interstate 29, tree damage in Aberdeen, snapped telephone poles in Milbank and damage to a carport near Britton.  Numerous photos of blowing dust were also sent to the National Weather Service social media pages as winds easily topped 50 mph. Highest gusts of 64 mph were observed by Department of Transportation weather stations in Harrold and Lees Corner. The Automated Weather Observing System in Britton also recorded a 62 mph gust.

Clusters of thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates from 2 to 4 inches per hour developed by midday on June 20, and despite being transient, continued to redevelop through the evening and into the overnight hours.  The primary focus occurred along Highway 18 in the afternoon southwest and south of Sioux Falls, then along Interstate 90 during the evening, and settled again to along the Highway 18 corridor as the effective cold pool boundary collapsed southward through the night.  The final 24 hour rainfall totals reached 6 to 12 inches by daybreak from eastern Hutchinson County eastward through Turner and Lincoln counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall through the early to mid-afternoon, which totaled 3 to 4 inches, yielded runoff which inundated several rural roads with significant flowing water. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for areal and river flooding for Lincoln County.

A deep low pressure to the south resulted in strong easterly winds across central, north central and northeast South Dakota late Friday night through the early morning hours of Sunday, with the bulk of the high winds being recorded on Saturday, April 6th. These strong winds were responsible for at least one small fire in Spink county, a vehicle accident on Interstate 29, tree damage in Aberdeen, snapped telephone poles in Milbank and damage to a carport near Britton.  Numerous photos of blowing dust were also sent to the National Weather Service social media pages as winds easily topped 50 mph. Highest gusts of 64 mph were observed by Department of Transportation weather stations in Harrold and Lees Corner. The Automated Weather Observing System in Britton also recorded a 62 mph gust.

Low pressure south of the region, and high pressure to the east helped generate strong east winds across north central South Dakota during the afternoon of April 15th, with a slight lull in the late evening and early overnight hours, before a period of stronger winds developed during the early morning hours of April 16th.  Peak winds during the day included gusts of 60 mph 4 miles east southeast of Akaska, and 59 mph 5 miles southwest of Java. During the early morning hours, winds peaked at 77 mph 9 miles south southeast of Spink Colony and 73 mph 7 miles south of Claremont. This second round of strong winds, generally between 60 and 70 mph, were mainly confined to the James valley, though sporadic gusts in excess of 58 mph were noted in north central and central South Dakota during that time as well. The strongest winds were also responsible for some tree and outbuilding damage in Ferney, as well as tree damage in and around Aberdeen.

A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches with pockets in excess of 15 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating several towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Aurora, Bon Homme, Brule, Charles Mix, Clay, Davison, Douglas, Gregory, Hanson, Hutchinson, Lake, Lincoln, McCook, Miner, Minnehaha, Moody, Sanborn, Turner, Union, and Yankton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Mitchell rose above flood stage the morning of June 21st, cresting around 6.72 feet above flood stage (23.72 feet) on June 23rd.  This crest is in the Moderate Stage. The river stayed above flood stage into July. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Hanson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts across Clay County from June 20-22 ranged from around a third of an inch across southern areas to 1 to 2 inches across northern areas. Farther north and upstream, 6 to 10 inches of rain fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, with locally higher amounts in excess of 12 inches. This rainfall resulted in record flooding along the Vermillion River and extensive overland flooding along the adjoining flood plain.||Two primary residences were impacted by flood waters across the northern portion of the county with each reporting more than three feet of water in their basements. One owner along the Vermillion River lost 100 percent of their land while countless other acres of farmland were lost in the vicinity of the river. One farmstead alone lost 60 plus acres. ||Many county and township roads across the northern portion of the county experienced flood damage ranging from gravel wash to major impacts. A couple concrete box culverts and bridges were destroyed by backup from the Vermillion River. Preliminary damage assessments estimate loss to public infrastructure to be 500 thousand dollars. ||Within the city of Vermillion, voluntary evacuations were activated near Saginaw and Missouri Streets. Cotton Park experienced extensive flood impacts, including major damage to about one mile of trail and a destroyed frisbee golf course, estimated to cost 1.5 million dollars. The Vermillion wastewater treatment plant also experienced significant damage, forcing the release of untreated water into the Vermillion River. Damage to the plant is estimated at 4.5 million dollars.||For additional information on river flooding, please see other flood entries for Clay County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates from 2 to 4 inches per hour developed by midday on June 20, and despite being transient, continued to redevelop through the evening and into the overnight hours.  The primary focus occurred along Highway 18 in the afternoon southwest and south of Sioux Falls, then along Interstate 90 during the evening, and settled again to along the Highway 18 corridor as the effective cold pool boundary collapsed southward through the night.  The final 24 hour rainfall totals reached 6 to 12 inches by daybreak from eastern Hutchinson County eastward through Turner and Lincoln counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After as much as 3 to 5 inches of rainfall between 8 pm and 11 pm, flash flooding pushed water up to homes and flooded basements in many portions of the city of Mitchell. No travel was recommended with numerous stranded motorists, and portions of Interstate 90 were closed due to water flowing over the road. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for areal and river flooding for Davison County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates from 2 to 4 inches per hour developed by midday on June 20, and despite being transient, continued to redevelop through the evening and into the overnight hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville.  Winds gusting to 30 to 35 mph resulted in several hours of visibility less than one mile in the late morning and early afternoon of January 12.

A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.7 inches 5 miles northeast of Salem and 2.0 inches at Bridgewater and Montrose.  Winds gusting up to 30 mph resulted in several hours of visibility around one mile in the early morning of January 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist boundary layer, combined with weak surface flow and a strong temperature inversion aloft resulted in areas of dense fog with visibility at times below one-quarter mile across southeast South Dakota on January 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On January 26, a young girl was crossing South Dakota Highway 34 about 10 miles west of Wessington Springs to board a school bus when she was struck by a vehicle. The vehicle engaged its breaks when flashing lights on the bus became more visible in dense fog and then slid on the icy road, hitting the young girl. The girl sustained serious, life-threatening injuries and succumbed to those injuries (indirect fatality) on February 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure in central South Dakota helped to support a broad complex of weak thunderstorms, one of which collapsed and generated a 65 mph wind gust at the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet 5 miles southwest of Java.

The gradient between low pressure to the west and high pressure to the east created strong south winds through the course of Thursday, September 12th. A peak wind gust of 59 mph was observed at the South Dakota Department of Transportation mesonet station 23 miles south southeast of Eagle Butte. Additionally, 16 other weather stations scattered across central and north central South Dakota recorded a peak wind gust in excess of 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches with pockets in excess of 15 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River at Vermillion rose above flood stage the morning of June 23rd, cresting around 12.15 feet above flood stage (33.15 feet) on June 24th. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Clay County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 52 mph at Brookings and 51 mph at Volga. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dissipating stratiform region from a large thunderstorm complex moved across southeast South Dakota in the early to mid-morning hours of May 21.  A wake low developed which produced a period of damaging wind gusts collocated with ending of rainfall while the feature lifted northeast across southeast South Dakota.  Wind gusts were measured frequently from 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained from 40 to 49 mph with a peak measured gust of 71 mph at a South Dakota Road Weather Information System site 6 miles west of Wagner. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast South Dakota was on the initial development end of a second mesoscale convective system in just two days as a frontal boundary remained anchored just south of the area. Scattered storms produced some spotty wind gusts as well as locally flooding rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day significant heavy rainfall event from June 20-22 resulted in widespread rain totals between 5 and 10 inches with pockets in excess of 15 inches across portions of southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota. As rainfall reached area streams and rivers, major to record flooding was observed, devastating several towns and communities. Extensive overland flooding also resulted in significant impacts and damages. All river crest information is preliminary until made official by the gauge owner.||A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Aurora, Bon Homme, Brule, Charles Mix, Clay, Davison, Douglas, Gregory, Hanson, Hutchinson, Lake, Lincoln, McCook, Miner, Minnehaha, Moody, Sanborn, Turner, Union, and Yankton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland rose above flood stage the evening of June 21st, cresting around 7.28 feet above flood stage (20.28 feet) on June 24th.  This crest is in the Major Stage. The river stayed above flood stage into July. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for Yankton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 50 mph near Beresford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground through the day near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN. An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop during the evening hours, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds peeled part of a roof from an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds developed during the early morning hours across portions of the northern Black Hills and adjacent foothills. Some wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were recorded across the area from Lead to Spearfish and Sturgis.
A thunderstorm briefly became severe east of Oelrichs, producing wind gusts up to 65 mph.
A thunderstorm became severe over central portions of Perkins County, producing wind gusts to over 60 mph.
A thunderstorm became severe south of Rapid City and moved east-northeast into eastern Pennington County. The storm produced wind gusts to over 60 mph along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe south of Rapid City and moved east-northeast into eastern Pennington County. The storm produced wind gusts to over 60 mph along its path.

A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall.  Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.8 inches at Mitchell. Visibility was as low as 1/4 to 1/2 mile at times of falling snow and wind gusts up to around 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall.  Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.2 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington and 2.6 inches at Huron.  Visibility was as low as 1 mile at times of falling snow and wind gusts to around 25 mph in the late morning and early afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -49 degrees Letcher and -48 degrees near Forestburg during the afternoon of January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast South Dakota was on the initial development end of a second mesoscale convective system in just two days as a frontal boundary remained anchored just south of the area. Scattered storms produced some spotty wind gusts as well as locally flooding rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts just ahead of the storm knocked numerous branches down across the yard. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually intense upper-level trough and surface low was located across southern Canada and moved toward the northern Great Lakes with a very deep mixed-layer across the Northern Plains. Synoptic winds gusted mainly 45 to 55 mph, with a few spots reaching around 60 mph in southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong synoptic winds gusted up to 49 mph near Forestburg and 46 mph near Letcher. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought fairly widespread precipitation to western South Dakota. Temperatures became cold enough for rain to change over to snow during the afternoon of the 29th, then persisted into the morning hours of the 30th. The heaviest snow fell during the afternoon and evening hours. Snowfall amounts were highly elevation dependent, with a band of heavier snow passing across the northern Black Hills during the afternoon and early evening. Snowfall amounts of 6 to over 10 inches were common from the Lead and Deadwood area to Cheyenne Crossing, Terry Peak, and O'Neill Pass. Amounts up to 6 inches were noted over the rest of the northern and central Black Hills into the northern foothills. Travel was impacted across portions of the Black Hills, especially the higher elevations of the northern Black Hills.

Areas ahead of a weak frontal boundary exhibited pooling of moisture with dew point readings into the upper 70s to lower 80s.  As temperatures reached the lower to mid 90s, dangerous heat conditions developed during the afternoon hours with heat index values from 100 to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat index values reached 102 degrees at a personal weather station near Utica.

A narrow band of elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a low-level jet, with around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and 50 knots of effective shear.  The exposed cell on the south end of the band was the strongest and produced up to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A considerable amount of quarter size and smaller hail fell along with heavy rainfall, which resulted in significant damage to the corn crop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of elevated thunderstorms developed on the nose of a low-level jet, with around 2000 J/kg MUCAPE and 50 knots of effective shear.  The exposed cell on the south end of the band was the strongest and produced up to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large amount of hail up to the size of quarters fell for 10 to 15 minutes, and drifts of hail remained through 0735 CST. Corn fields were badly stripped. Rainfall of 2.95 inches accompanied the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fire sparked in a farmers field in North Dakota spread rapidly southeast due to ambient dry conditions and strong northwest winds, with the nearby Department of Transportation mesonet 5 miles west northwest of McLaughlin recording a peak wind gust of 49 mph.  Approximately 1600 acres of grass and farmland were burned by the fire, which managed to partially cross portions of South Dakota highway 63 before being extinguished.

A few thunderstorms developed during the evening along a weak surface trough with up to 1500 J/kg MLCAPE. Weak deep-layer shear limited organization potential, with merging cold pools quickly reducing the intensity of storms along the line.

The thermal cap diurnally weakened along a weak surface trough from north central Nebraska into central South Dakota by very late afternoon, which allowed an isolated strong to severe thunderstorm to develop. The storm tracked slowly eastward through the early evening. Very large hail occurred early in the life cycle, with a transition toward damaging wind and torrential rainfall as it shifted east of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of windows were broken in a camper and holes punched in siding due to wind-driven hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October was the 8th warmest on record for Pierre, with only 0.01 inches of moisture. Pierre also experienced its 2nd warmest September on record. The lack of moisture and abnormally warm temperatures resulted in expansion of Severe/D2 conditions across Lyman, Buffalo and Jones counties.

September featured record warmth with the ASOS at Mobridge recording a record warm average temperature for the month, including tying/exceeding a record high 3 times in the last week of September. This combined with a lack of moisture, with only 0.01 inches of precipitation recorded for the entire month of September in Mobridge, set the stage for the development and expansion of D2/Severe Drought across north central South Dakota at the start of October. Conditions did not improve during the month either, with Mobridge then seeing the 3rd warmest average temperature, with 2 additional record high temperatures observed, and only an additional 0.08 inches of moisture.

The thermal cap diurnally weakened along a weak surface trough from north central Nebraska into central South Dakota by very late afternoon, which allowed an isolated strong to severe thunderstorm to develop. The storm tracked slowly eastward through the early evening. Very large hail occurred early in the life cycle, with a transition toward damaging wind and torrential rainfall as it shifted east of the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts up to 65 mph were measured by personal weather station KSDPLATT39.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, spurred by a ridge-rider mid-level wave across North Dakota,  formed generally near a weak surface convergence boundary across far northern Nebraska and southeast South Dakota and a surface front over central and northeastern South Dakota. The storms weakened before moving too far eastward due to a strong thermal cap, but this warm and drier layer allowed storms to briefly produce some stronger winds east of the James River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe/D2 drought conditions has expanded to cover many counties across northeast South Dakota. While dry conditions started in August, the climate for September was a much more extreme anomaly, with Sisseton recording its warmest average temperature for the month, Aberdeen its 2nd and Watertown its 3rd. Sisseton recorded its 3rd driest September on record, while Watertown observed its 6th. These ambient dry conditions continued into October, with Watertown and Aberdeen observing their 4th and 6th driest Octobers on record, respectively. In addition to October being abnormally dry; Aberdeen, Sisseton and Watertown would all see average temperatures for the month at least 6 degrees above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated, strong storm moving from between just north of Mobridge to just southwest of Mound City generated a strong tornado that was well documented by storm chasers and spotters. Additional, weaker satellite tornadoes and embedded vortices within the main tornado were observed during the 30 minute duration this tornado was on the ground. This resulted in sporadic, intense areas of multi-directional tornado damage, as well as areas of very broad uniform damage. After the main tornado had dissipated, a distinct additional tornado developed southeast of Mound City lasting a few minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down roughly 4.5 miles southeast of Mound City, SD in open crop and grassland. Photo and video evidence of the tornado was documented. The track was also noted on high resolution satellite imagery.

High heat and humidity moved into the region during the early days of August. High temperatures in the upper 90s to low 100s were widespread across central, north central and northeast South Dakota on both August 2nd and 3rd. A peak high temperature of 104 degrees was recorded at the South Dakota State University Mesonet stations 7 miles southwest of Hayes and 16 miles east southeast of Pierre on August 3rd.  Dewpoints across much of northeast South Dakota were also well into the 70s with a few locations topping an 80 degree dewpoint. Heat index values reached 110 degrees at Stratford and Millette on Friday August 2nd and 113 degrees in Redfield on Saturday August 3rd.

Another hot and humid airmass moved into the region in late August. Heat index values topped out at 114 degrees at the Department of Transportation weather station 5 miles east of Orient, with an additional 30 weather stations across 16 counties in central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index above 105 degrees that afternoon. This was thanks to temperatures in the upper 90s and dewpoints up to 80 degrees. Aberdeen would also see a record warm low temperature of 75 degrees that morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread, slow moving thunderstorms brought hail, gusty wnds and heavy rain to portions of central South Dakota on the afternoon and evening of August 21st. Hail to the size of tennis balls was observed by a trained spotter 4 miles northwest of Murdo. A peak  wind speed of 65 mph was observed at a weather station 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc while the ASOS in Pierre peaked at 59 mph. Emergency management and trained spotters estimated about 3.5 inches of rainfall associated with the storms in portions of Jones and Hyde counties which resulted in water over a few roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3 to 4 inch deep water over the roadway on highway 14 in Fort Pierre.

Another hot and humid airmass moved into the region in late August. Heat index values topped out at 114 degrees at the Department of Transportation weather station 5 miles east of Orient, with an additional 30 weather stations across 16 counties in central and northeast South Dakota reporting a heat index above 105 degrees that afternoon. This was thanks to temperatures in the upper 90s and dewpoints up to 80 degrees. Aberdeen would also see a record warm low temperature of 75 degrees that morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated, strong storm moving from between just north of Mobridge to just southwest of Mound City generated a strong tornado that was well documented by storm chasers and spotters. Additional, weaker satellite tornadoes and embedded vortices within the main tornado were observed during the 30 minute duration this tornado was on the ground. This resulted in sporadic, intense areas of multi-directional tornado damage, as well as areas of very broad uniform damage. After the main tornado had dissipated, a distinct additional tornado developed southeast of Mound City lasting a few minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:50 pm CDT, the tornado touched down on the west side of|South Dakota Highway 1804, just north of 118th St. Within a few minutes of being|on the ground, it tracked east and crossed South Dakota Highway 1804, tossing|hay bales in its path. Between 8:00-8:05 pm CDT, the tornado|continued in a more southeast direction, crossing 118th St and|encountering an electric transmission line, resulting in the|collapse of three electrical transmission towers. The tornado|then continued east-southeast toward 119th St, where it|encountered a farmstead. At this location, multiple trees were|broken, snapped, and uprooted. One small shed was lifted, another|had its walls collapse, and a machine shed had its doors blown|out. The main house lost windows, and a tractor lost its doors|facing east and west, although the front and back windshields|remained intact. Additionally, a trailer was rolled onto the|road, and other farm implements were destroyed or moved. Between|8:05-8:10 pm CDT, the tornado would cross to the south of 119th|St, flipping a haybine to the west and through a fence. The main|tornadic circulation broadened and tracked east, becoming nearly|stationary at times as it traversed over cropland between 299th|and 301st Ave. Many smaller tornadic vortices were|noted on video accounts during this period. Toward the end of the tornado|cycle, between roughly 8:15-8:20 pm CDT, it intensified once|again, becoming narrower and tracking from southwest to|northeast, crossing 119th St between 301st and 302nd Ave. The|track was clearly seen through a corn crop before lifting. Around|this same time, a distinct smaller tornado/vortex damaged a|farmstead. At this location, a tree was uprooted, a silo was|dented, and two shop buildings lost roof panels and doors.|Additionally, a travel trailer was rolled 100 feet across 119th|St, with debris tossed from north-northeast to south-southwest|into a cornfield. Two other smaller tornadic circulations were|also noted in the corn crop just west of the intersection of|302nd Ave and 119th St.

A slow moving storm and heavy downpour resulted in urban flooding within the city of Pierre. The COOP observer in town recorded 2.84 inches of rain, while the ASOS at the Airport recorded 1.68 inches during a 2 hour span. Pooling of water resulted in both streets and sidewalks under water in portions of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread, slow moving thunderstorms brought hail, gusty wnds and heavy rain to portions of central South Dakota on the afternoon and evening of August 21st. Hail to the size of tennis balls was observed by a trained spotter 4 miles northwest of Murdo. A peak  wind speed of 65 mph was observed at a weather station 13 miles north northwest of Sansarc while the ASOS in Pierre peaked at 59 mph. Emergency management and trained spotters estimated about 3.5 inches of rainfall associated with the storms in portions of Jones and Hyde counties which resulted in water over a few roads.
Severe thunderstorms over Butte County produced wind gusts to around 70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening heat index values reached as high as 104 degrees at Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and upper-level shortwave brought gusty northwest winds and colder air to the area. The gusty winds and moisture in place helped to produce a prolonged period of heavy upslope snowfall during the morning and afternoon hours across the northern Black Hills and foothills. Amounts of 6 to around 12 inches were common across the northern slopes of the Black Hills into the higher foothills. Amounts of 2 to around 6 inches fell over the rest of the northern Black Hills and northern foothills into portions of the west central South Dakota plains. The heavy snow impacted travel across the northern Black Hills area during the daytime and evening hours.

Pockets of light freezing rain moved across northeast South Dakota between the mornings of December 9th and December 10th. Very little moisture was observed, however it was enough to make the roads extremely slippery, resulting in multiple vehicle accidents. One such accident claimed the life of an 81 year old commuter traveling on a rural highway 2 miles east of Milbank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi lost control due to icy roads along US highway 12, 2 miles east of Andover where the highway curves, and ended up temporarily partially blocking the highway.

Wind gusts of 60 to around 70 mph were recorded during the overnight and morning hours. Winds started to taper off in most areas by the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2/Severe drought continued across portions of north central and south central South Dakota. Mobridge and Timber lake would only see 0.26 inches of total moisture for December, which is about half of normal in comparison to climatology for these sites. Some areas did see relief in central South Dakota, however the locations encompassed within Novembers' D2/Severe drought area that follow the White River failed to receive appreciable moisture. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of light freezing rain moved across northeast South Dakota between the mornings of December 9th and December 10th. Very little moisture was observed, however it was enough to make the roads extremely slippery, resulting in multiple vehicle accidents. One such accident claimed the life of an 81 year old commuter traveling on a rural highway 2 miles east of Milbank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice on US highway 212 resulted in a package delivery service vehicle sliding out of control into a farmers field just west of the town of Henry.

The SDSU mesonet station 12 miles south of Vivian would also see 0.26 inches of moisture in December, which again is only about half of climatology.

Low pressure that moved across the state produced some very light freezing rain and snow. The resulting icy roads, in combination with strong winds, resulted in several slide-ins and vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota Highway Patrol issued a warning to commuters about icy and snow cover roads resulting in hazardous travel for portions of highway 212 near Watertown, as a semi had recently jack-knifed in the ditch between the east and westbound lanes. Total snowfall for the Watertown area recorded by a CoCoRaHS observer only amounted to 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of light freezing rain moved across northeast South Dakota between the mornings of December 9th and December 10th. Very little moisture was observed, however it was enough to make the roads extremely slippery, resulting in multiple vehicle accidents. One such accident claimed the life of an 81 year old commuter traveling on a rural highway 2 miles east of Milbank.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 81 year old man died in a single-vehicle crash at approximately 2:38 pm at the intersection of 149th St and 481st Ave. The vehicle was traveling on 149th St when it slid on the slick roadway, crossed into the ditch and rolled into a field. The driver was not wearing a seatbelt and ejected from the vehicle resulting in fatal injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds followed a low pressure system as it moved across the region. A peak wind gust of 68 mph was observed by the Department of Transportation weather station near Ridgeview and by a personal weather station 5 miles west southwest of Mound City. The ASOS at Pierre Regional Airport recorded a peak gust of 66 mph. 7 other weather stations scattered across central and north central South Dakota would see a peak wind gust in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An empty silo at Dakota Mill and Grain in Murdo collapsed as a result of strong winds estimated in excess of 60 mph at approximately 10:15 in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level system slowly crossed the region, bringing snowfall to the northern Black Hills area. A series of disturbances within the trough brought periods of snowfall over a 2 to 3 day period. The highest snowfall amounts were over the higher elevations of the northern Black Hills, especially favored upslope locations. Snowfall totals of 5 to 12 inches were common across the area, with amounts to near 18 inches over the higher elevations from Cheyenne Crossing to O'Neill Pass. Amounts of 3 to 6 inches were common across much of the northern foothills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of southeast South Dakota were on the southern edge of a band of snowfall which brushed areas north of Interstate 90, and was heaviest from 3 to 6 inches closer to Highway 14.  A majority of poor visibility conditions as low as one mile or less was caused by the snowfall which was largely ahead of the stronger winds with this system.  Winds were strongest through the Missouri River corridor with gusts as high as 60 to 70 mph, while closer to 35 to 50 mph toward southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained from 30 to 35 mph at times, with gusts as high as 57 mph at Wolsey, 55 mph at Huron and 48 mph at Cavour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure moved across western South Dakota through Nebraska, cool air pushed into the region and changed a period of rainfall during the morning to light to moderate snowfall during the late morning and afternoon.  Snowfall was generally from 1 to 3 inches near and east of the Missouri River, with up to 5 inches across the higher elevation of Gregory County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches near Academy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system generated widespread strong winds. A peak wind gust of 69 mph was observed by the Pierre Regional Airport ASOS, while 68 mph gusts were recorded by mesonet weather stations 1 mile northeast of Ridgeview in Dewey county, 10 miles southwest of Chantier Creek rec area in Stanley county and 10 miles south of Bullhead in Corson county. Additionally, another 24 weather stations scattered across central, north central and northeast South Dakota would observer a peak wind gust in excess of 60 mph. These strong winds caused a partly constructed framework of a storage building to collapse on the west side of Aberdeen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee of the Weather Service reported that the framing of a partly constructed storage building had collapsed due to high winds. The peak wind gust at the Aberdeen regional airport ASOS, about 2 miles to the south southeast of the incident, was measured at 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak system generated a dusting to up to 2 inches of snow and some light freezing rain by the early afternoon of  December 17th.  The resulting icy roads were a contributing factor in at least one vehicle accident with injuries in northeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two-vehicle crash resulting in injuries was reported by the Hamlin County Sheriff along a stretch of SD Highway 22, which was slick due to light ice and snow. The accident caused the highway to be closed for nearly eight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low passed slowly across the northern high Plains, bringing a prolonged period of strong winds at times across northwest into south central South Dakota. The strongest winds were generally during the late morning and afternoon hours of the 19th and again during the overnight into morning hours on the 20th. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were noted across much of these areas with difficult travel at times, especially for high profile vehicles.

Rainfall varied from near normal to almost 250 percent of normal during the month, with the least precipitation over portions of far southeast South Dakota and toward the Missouri River in south central South Dakota. Heavier precipitation mid-month led to improvement in drought conditions in the far east by late month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought persisted through November with precipitation varying from 100 to 200 percent of normal with a majority falling on a single day mid-month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across southeast South Dakota.  Some limited moisture wrapped back southward around the area of low pressure and produced a narrow band of light to briefly moderate snowfall during the overnight and early morning hours of November 20 with a small area between 1 and 3.5 inches accumulation.  At times of falling snow, the very strong winds gusting from 40 to 65 mph produced some brief visibility less than one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds occasionally gusted as high as 53 mph near Forestburg, 51 mph near Woonsocket, and 48 mph near Letcher.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought improved to moderate (D1) conditions after around 200 percent of normal monthly precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across southeast South Dakota.  Some limited moisture wrapped back southward around the area of low pressure and produced a narrow band of light to briefly moderate snowfall during the overnight and early morning hours of November 20 with a small area between 1 and 3.5 inches accumulation.  At times of falling snow, the very strong winds gusting from 40 to 65 mph produced some brief visibility less than one mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to briefly moderate snow fell in a very narrow band over the central and western portions of the county which totaled 2.1 inches at Howard.  The snow was accompanied by strong northwest winds which gusted as high as 50 mph near Carthage and 45 mph near Howard and resulted in occasional visibilities less than one mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2/Severe drought conditions carried over from October, however a pair of systems brought widespread moisture to the region between November 13th and November 19th. That, and additional light amounts of moisture scattered though the month amounted to a combined 2.54 inches measured by the COOP observer in Milbank, while the COOP observers in Watertown and Sisseton recorded 2.32 and 1.84, respectively.

A strong upper-level low passed slowly across the northern high Plains, bringing a prolonged period of strong winds at times across northwest into south central South Dakota. The strongest winds were generally during the late morning and afternoon hours of the 19th and again during the overnight into morning hours on the 20th. Wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were noted across much of these areas with difficult travel at times, especially for high profile vehicles.

A deep low pressure system generated widespread strong winds. A peak wind gust of 69 mph was observed by the Pierre Regional Airport ASOS, while 68 mph gusts were recorded by mesonet weather stations 1 mile northeast of Ridgeview in Dewey county, 10 miles southwest of Chantier Creek rec area in Stanley county and 10 miles south of Bullhead in Corson county. Additionally, another 24 weather stations scattered across central, north central and northeast South Dakota would observer a peak wind gust in excess of 60 mph. These strong winds caused a partly constructed framework of a storage building to collapse on the west side of Aberdeen.
